9.1.label: Question 9.1
9.1.scenario: A developed country is considering changing its approach to taxing individual income protection insurance. Currently, premiums and benefits are both paid tax-free. It is considering changing this so that all benefits are taxed as income for policyholders who claim. Premiums will continue to be tax-free.
9.1.question: Discuss the implications of this change for policyholders and insurers.

Existing policyholders - claiming: Any change will have the most significant impact on policyholders currently claiming benefit. [½]
These individuals may see their benefit level drop suddenly which could cause financial difficulties. [½]
Currently, individuals will receive any benefits on IP insurance gross of tax, so the sum assured an individual will need will be the amount required to cover their living costs on incapacity. [½]
If this change is implemented, the benefits received will reduce for many policyholders as income tax will be payable on this amount. [½]
Although, the exact impact will depend on the tax position of the individual and the tax regime in the country. [½]
An individual may continue to receive the same benefit amount as under the previous system if their total income is less than any tax-free threshold. [½]
Alternatively, they may only incur tax on some of the benefit if there are stepped rates of tax. [½]
The government and tax authority should consider the potential negative impact on individuals and their dependents as a result of this change. [½]
It could consider implementing this change only for new IP insurance sales, [½]
However, this may cause practical issues in working out which policies should be taxed and which shouldn't. [½]
Existing policyholders – not claiming: Existing policyholders who are not claiming may be given the option to increase their level of cover to ensure their benefit is sufficient once tax is deducted. [½]
The insurer will need to consider the additional selective risk of offering this option to existing lives if the insurer offers this without requiring further medical underwriting. [½]
Care will also need to be taken around policyholders within the deferred period. These individuals could be treated as existing claimants for the purposes of consistency. [½]
New policyholders: New policies will have higher sums assured than previous cover and therefore premiums are likely to increase. [½]
This may also reduce demand for individual IP insurance in the future. [½]
This may lead to increased risks of anti-selection for the insurer in relation to new individual business. [½]
Insurer: If the change was only implemented for new business, prior to the new regime being implemented, insurers may receive increased volumes of new business from individuals trying to take advantage of the old regime. [½]
These applications may have a higher risk of anti-selection. [½]
This will also incur additional new business and administrative strain for the insurer. [½]
Insurers could look to increase existing claimants sum assured (or replacement ratio) so that the net income remains the same as the current level of benefit. [½]
However, this would lead to the insurers incurring losses on these policies. [½]
These losses could be recouped by increasing new business premiums, however, this may reduce new business volumes. [½]
The insurer may need to increase any limits on the level of cover for new business, eg maximum replacement ratios. [½]
Higher benefits and premiums may lead to higher commission payments being due for new business sales. [½]
The insurer may need to reprice its product. [½]
There may be higher demand for group IP insurance in the future, depending on how this product is taxed. [½]
